q: When is the Patch function the RIGHT choice over an edit form in a canvas app?
o: When you must change data programmatically without user interaction, or the design has no form
Whenever you display records in a gallery
Only for reading a single record's columns
Any time you navigate between screens
why: Patch updates data sources directly, ideal for repetitive/programmatic writes or designs without forms.

q: Which function removes ALL records from a collection while keeping its columns defined?
o: Clear
RemoveIf
Remove
DropColumns
why: Clear empties a collection but preserves its column structure; Remove/RemoveIf target specific rows.

q: Decide whether each statement about Patch, Remove and collections is true.

rows.s: Remove deletes a record without asking the user to confirm.
Patch, Remove and RemoveIf can target a collection as well as a tabular data source.
Patch and Remove can each modify many records at once on their own.

why: Patch/Remove act on one record; for many you loop with ForAll. Both work on collections and tables, and Remove gives no confirmation.

q: You need to write each row of one table into another, one record per source row. Which approach fits?
o: ForAll looping a Patch/Remove, or Collect to copy rows between tables
A single Remove call
Setting a global variable
The Clear function
why: Bulk changes use ForAll (per-record Patch/Remove) or Collect to append each source row to a target table.

q: In Patch(CustomerOrders, Defaults(CustomerOrders), {Status:"Pending"}), what does Defaults() do?
o: Signals Patch to CREATE a new record
Deletes the matching record first
Validates the column types
Filters existing rows before writing
why: Passing Defaults(DataSource) as the base record tells Patch to create a new row; passing an existing record edits it.

q: Decide whether each Patch column rule is true.

rows.s: Column names in Patch are case-sensitive.
You must supply all REQUIRED columns of the data source.
You can write a string value into a number column as long as it looks numeric.
You may set as many or as few OPTIONAL columns as you want.

why: Patch needs correct casing, all required columns, and matching data types; optional columns are up to you.

q: Which are valid sources for the values you pass into a Patch change record? (choose 2)
o: A control property such as Dropdown1.Selected.Value
The output of a formula like User().FullName
The name of the screen you are on
The app's file size
why: Patch values can come from hardcoded literals, variables, control properties or formula outputs.

q: Patch(CustomerOrders, ThisItem, {Region:"Asia", Country:"China"}) is run on an existing row. What happens to prior Region/Country values?
o: They are overwritten with the new values
They are appended to a history log
The row is duplicated with the new values
Patch is rejected because the row exists
why: Passing an existing record edits it in place, overwriting the specified columns.

q: You must empty a data source's records AND immediately reload a fresh set into a collection in one step. Which function is designed for that?
o: ClearCollect
Clear then a separate Collect on a timer
RemoveIf with true
DropColumns
why: ClearCollect combines Clear and Collect in a single call, ideal for reloading a collection all at once.

q: For a shopping-cart collection where the user may empty the cart WITHOUT immediately adding new items, which pattern fits best?
o: Clear now and Collect later as separate steps
A single ClearCollect
RemoveIf(cart, false)
Patch with Defaults
why: Multi-step scenarios (clear now, add later) use separate Clear and Collect; all-at-once reloads use ClearCollect.

q: RemoveIf(CustomerOrders, true) is executed. What is the result?
o: Every record is deleted because the condition is always true
Nothing, because true is not a valid filter
Only the first record is deleted
The columns are removed but rows remain
why: RemoveIf tests each row against the condition; a condition of true matches all rows, deleting them (no undo).

q: Decide whether each statement about deleting data is true.

rows.s: Power Apps provides a built-in undo / recycle bin for records deleted via RemoveIf.
Setting the condition to true also works with the Filter function for troubleshooting.

why: There is no undo in Power Apps for deletes; recovery depends on the data source. A true condition is a handy Filter debug trick.
